sections.heading: Initial Report in the Field of Dentistry
INITIAL REPORT IN THE FIELD OF DENTISTRY

elements.text: WCAB:		POM
Case #:		ADJ17023256
Date of Injury: 	11/05/2021
Date of current exam: 11/26/2025
Interpreter:
Patient
Last Name: 		Anderson
First Name: 		Ronald
Sex: 			MALE
Date of Birth: 	08/03/1955
Occupation: 		Site Inspector
Address:
City:
State:
Zip:
Mr. Anderson states he worked as a Site Inspector for Smith Emery Company. He worked 8 hours per day, 5 days per week.

Mr. Anderson states his job entailed prolonged driving, walking, standing, squatting, twisting, bending forward, side bending, reaching below shoulder level, reaching above shoulder level, pushing, pulling, gripping, grasping, lifting up to a maximum of 40 lbs, carrying up to a maximum of 40 lbs.
Mr. Ronald Anderson is disabled and has not returned to work since November 5, 2021.
Mr. Anderson states that on 11/05/2021, he was involved in an industrial injury in the course of performing his usual and customary job duties.
Mr. Anderson reports that he was handling bulky items and injuring his customer, when he slipped and fell onto a hard panel with his left leg striking a pile of lumber.
He reports that as a result of this incident, he sustained injuries to his lower back, left leg, and left knee.
Mr. Anderson rates the severity of pain in his lower back as 6 out of 10, in his left leg as 7 out of 10, and in his left knee as 8 out of 10, all on a VAS.

Mr. Anderson finds that in response to his industrial related orthopedic pain, he developed emotional stressors. He finds he is clenching his teeth and bracing his facial musculature in response to his orthopedic pain, and also in response to the resultant emotional stressors experienced. This has caused him to develop facial and jaw pain.
Mr. Ronald Anderson states he has received the following treatments due to the industrial injury: surgery on his L Ankles.
Mr. Anderson states his past medical history includes: high blood pressure, diabetes, stomach/acid reflux, and breathing problems. He denies significant other past medical history.
Mr. Ronald Anderson underwent a right lumbar surgery approximately 30 years ago.
Mr. Anderson denies prior injuries.
Mr. Ronald Anderson denies any injuries after the date of industrial injury.
Mr. Anderson states he does not smoke and has never smoked. He denies use of recreational drugs. Mr. Anderson is right hand dominant.

Mr. Anderson states his last dental visit was 3-4 years ago. He states his teeth were last cleaned 3-4 years ago. He reports having x-rays taken 1 month ago by Dr. Jerry Adams. Mr. Anderson does not have dental insurance. Dr. Schames is requesting the apportionment records from Mr. Anderson's dentist.
Subjective Complaints
Mr. Anderson reports headaches located in sharp areas, occurring less than 1% of the time. He rates the severity as 4-5 out of 10 on a Visual Analog Scale (VAS).
No complaints of right or left face pain.
Mr. Anderson reports clenching and grinding his teeth.
Mr. Anderson states that he has bad breath after the injury.
Mr./Ms. Ronald Anderson states he/she has a tendency to doze while:
The results of this test show a total score of 10, which suggests the presence of daytime drowsiness.
ACTIVITY | EXAMPLE | DIFFICULTY

sections.heading: Clinical Examination of the Temporomandibular Joint

elements.text: Mr. Ronald Anderson exhibited tenderness upon palpation of the right temporomandibular joint, with a reported VAS score of .
Clicking was noted in the right TMJ during the opening phase.
Upon translational and lateral movements of the mandible, clicking was palpated in the right TMJ.
Mr. Anderson exhibited tenderness upon palpation of the left temporomandibular joint, with a reported VAS score of .
Clicking was noted in the left TMJ during the opening phase.
Upon translational and lateral movements of the mandible, clicking was palpated in the left TMJ.
Maximum assisted opening was  mm.
Unassisted opening was  mm.
Right lateral excursion was  mm.
Left lateral excursion was  mm.
Protrusive movement was  mm.
Joint loading test of the right TMJ elicited .
Joint loading test of the left TMJ elicited .
Please Note: According to the State of California Disability Standards, the minimal normal range of motion of opening of the mouth is an interincisal opening of 38.1 mm.
Upon examination, Mr. Anderson was objectively found to exhibit:
Maximum interincisal opening of 48 mm painless
Maximum right lateral excursion of 8 mm painless
Maximum left lateral excursion of  mm painless
Maximum protrusion of 6 mm painless
Upon examination, I determined Mr. Anderson exhibits:
At maximum intercuspation:	Class I occlusion
Overbite:    3 mm
Overjet:      3 mm
centered
Missing Teeth
Objectively-disclosed bacterial biofilm deposits on his teeth and around his gum tissues
Objective Clinical Findings Confirming Bruxism/Clenching and Bracing of the Facial Musculature for Mr. Ronald Anderson
1. Moderate teeth indentations/scalloping on the lateral borders of his tongue were objectively noted.
2. Slight bite mark lines (linea alba) on the inside of his cheeks (buccal mucosa) were objectively documented.
3. Slight occlusal wear on the surfaces of his teeth was objectively noted.
4. The presence of biofilm on his teeth was objectively documented.
Ultrasonic Doppler auscultation of the temporomandibular joints was performed to assess joint sounds and internal derangement.
Upon examination of Mr. Ronald Anderson's right TMJ, no abnormal sounds were detected during translation or lateral movements.
Upon examination of his left TMJ, there were no recorded findings for translation or lateral movements.
The findings are consistent with no significant internal derangement or osteoarthritic changes being detected in the right TMJ.
Surface electromyography (sEMG) was performed bilaterally on the muscles of mastication.
Mr. Anderson's right temporalis muscle showed 4.2 microvolts at rest, 120.5 microvolts during maximum voluntary contraction, and 80.2 microvolts at peak activity.
His left temporalis muscle showed 4.5 microvolts at rest, 115.1 microvolts during maximum voluntary contraction, and 78.5 microvolts at peak activity.
The right masseter muscle showed 3.8 microvolts at rest, 100.2 microvolts during maximum voluntary contraction, and 70.5 microvolts at peak activity.
His left masseter muscle showed 4.1 microvolts at rest, 95.6 microvolts during maximum voluntary contraction, and 72.1 microvolts at peak activity.
Bilateral comparison revealed a slight asymmetry between the right and left temporalis muscles at rest, with the left side being 7.1% higher.
The elevated resting values for both temporalis and masseter muscles bilaterally suggest hypertonicity and possible bruxism.
The presence of incoordination and aberrant function is consistent with Mr. Anderson's clinical presentation.

The muscles of mastication were tested for their functional capacity measuring the maximum bite force and strength of these facial muscles. The measurements were objectively determined with the use of a gnathodynamometer. The muscles of mastication are innervated by the fifth cranial nerve, the Trigeminal Nerve. The motor function of the Trigeminal Nerve controls the bite force of the muscles of mastication.

Mr. Ronald Anderson's maximum bite force was objectively measured at 138 Newtons. This is significantly lower than expected when the normal amount of Newton forces to eat hard and chewy foods should exceed 400 Newtons. (400 Newtons = 90 Pounds Force). Mr. Anderson's complaint of difficulty eating hard and chewy foods is consistent with these objective findings.

The bite force on the right side was 76 Newtons, and on the left side was 62 Newtons. The objective measurements also confirm that Mr. Anderson has a weaker force of his bite on one side, with a difference of 14 Newtons between the right and left sides.

"Gnathodynamometer--a reliable device for assessing jaw bite force." Shushma, G., Kantly, R. Journal of Evolution of Medical and Dental Sciences, vol. 5, no. 102, 22 Dec. 2016, pp. 7488+. The findings from this diagnostic test will be correlated with the results from the temperature gradient studies to be discussed next.
Diagnostic Autonomic Nervous System Testing, consisting of Pulse Oximetry during the patient's performance of the Mueller Maneuver, revealed results within normal limits.
DIAGNOSTIC α-AMYLASE ENZYME ANALYSIS

sections.heading: Diagnostic Salivary Flow and Buffering Tests

elements.text: Diagnostic salivary flow and buffering tests revealed:
• Observable dry mouth, dry lips, cracked lips
• Viscous saliva

The standard of care objective test advocated by the American Dental Association to prove whether definite qualitative changes of the saliva are present or not was performed (and the results photographed). A tongue depressor adheres to the inside of Mr. Anderson's cheeks, which conclusively document with certainty that he does indeed have qualitative changes in his saliva, which has resulted in the increased surface tension of the salivary liquid. "In addition to conducting visual inspection, the dentist can use a tongue blade to assess tissue dryness..." (Journal of the American Dental Association, August 2003).

Stress, as well as the side effect of medications, causes xerostomia which manifests as qualitative changes of the saliva. Saliva in its proper form is anti-mutagenic which means it helps decrease the onset of oral cancer. When saliva has been quantitatively changed due to the side effect of medications and/or due to the anti-cholinergic side effect of stress, it has been scientifically documented
To be determined when Mr. Anderson returns to our Hawthorne, Reseda, and/or Anaheim offices for x-rays or presents us with x-rays taken at another dental office.

Diagnostic photographs document moderate scalloping on the lateral borders of Mr. Anderson's tongue and slight bite mark lines on the inside of his cheeks. The photographs also document slight wear on the surfaces of his teeth. Furthermore, the photographs document the Xerostomia / Anti-Cholinergic side effect where, due to qualitative changes of the saliva, a tongue depressor sticks to the inside of the cheek - even when not held by hand. The gum tissues appear normal with no signs of inflammation or bleeding. The photographs reveal objectively-disclosed bacterial biofilm deposits on Mr. Anderson's teeth and around his gum tissues.
After a review of Mr. Anderson's history and clinical examination, the diagnoses are:
1. F45.8 — Bruxism
2. M79.1 — Myalgia of Facial Muscles
3. M26.69 — Osteoarthritis of the TMJ

Upon examination, I found Mr. Anderson presents with objective clinical findings of bruxism, including tongue scalloping, cheek bite marks, and significant tooth wear on multiple teeth. These findings are consistent with a diagnosis of bruxism.

Palpation of the facial muscles revealed tenderness in the masseter and temporalis muscles bilaterally, with tenderness scores of 5 on both the left and right sides. The muscles were also found to have palpable taut bands, which is consistent with a diagnosis of myalgia. Mr. Anderson reports experiencing significant orthopedic pain, which he states has been a source of considerable stress for him. He has reported that his pain levels have increased since the industrial exposure.

Diagnostic testing revealed elevated muscular activity and dysfunction on sEMG testing. Bite force analysis showed values of 62 on the left and 76 on the right, indicating an imbalance in bite forces. Evaluation of Mr. Anderson's saliva reveals objective evidence of qualitative changes, as demonstrated by the tongue depressor test, and quantitative changes, with a stimulated salivary flow of less than 0.5mL and an unstimulated salivary flow of less than 0.1mL.

The scientific literature has documented that industrial exposure can lead to increased stress levels, which can contribute to bruxism. As noted in the literature, "stress and anxiety are significant contributing factors to bruxism" (1). Mr. Anderson's industrial exposure has resulted in significant orthopedic pain, which has contributed to his stress levels. He has reported that his pain levels have increased since the industrial exposure, stating that "the pain is always there and it's getting worse." This increased stress has likely contributed to his bruxism.

Phase IV clinical studies of the FDA data have documented that certain medications can cause bruxism as a side effect. Mr. Anderson is currently taking medications that have been associated with bruxism. The FDA data indicate that these medications "may cause bruxism" (2). However, it is with reasonable medical probability that the industrial exposure, rather than the medication alone, is the primary contributing factor to Mr. Anderson's bruxism.

Even if Mr. Anderson had prior episodes of bruxism, may not have been aware that he had prior episodes of bruxism, it is with reasonable medical probability that the industrial exposure would have aggravated any preexisting bruxism by at least 1%. There are numerous industrial-related factors that, with reasonable medical probability, would be contributing to, aggravating, accelerating, and/or lighting up his bruxism.

Periodontal examination findings revealed evidence of inflammation, including swelling and recession. Evaluation of Mr. Anderson's periodontal status reveals that there is significant inflammation present. Even if Mr. Anderson had prior inflammation of the gums, it is with reasonable medical probability that the industrial exposure has contributed to the aggravation of his periodontal condition.

Therefore, it is with reasonable medical probability that Mr. Anderson's industrial exposure is the primary cause of his bruxism and related dental damage. The objective clinical findings, diagnostic test results, and scientific literature all support this conclusion.

An examining doctor's opinion for causation must be based on a reliable differential diagnosis formed after taking a history, physical examination, diagnostic tests and, if available, a review of prior medical records. I have considered this for Mr. Anderson.
The reader must remember: Treatment is not apportioned!

sections.heading: Temporal Connection Between Industrial Exposure and Dental Conditions

elements.text: In my discussion section above, I discussed how, with reasonable medical probability, the industrial exposure and its resultant orthopedic pain and emotional stressors, have developed consequential and derivative injuries for Mr. Anderson in the area of Dentistry.

As explained above, even if the medical records reveal Mr. Anderson had these dental problems prior to the industrial exposure, it is with reasonable medical probability that his dental problems were contributed to and aggravated on an industrial basis by at least 1% in response to his industrial pain, any resultant stress, and the side effect of medication taken on an industrial related basis.

While I understand that one of the arguments a person may bring up is latency of when these dental problems started to occur, one has to understand that latency is individual and depends on the intensity of exposure and predilection of the individual.

I observed Mr. Anderson during the course of this evaluation. A comfortable doctor-patient relationship was established which enabled Mr. Anderson to candidly confide the difficulties he experienced as a result of his industrial injuries. I found Mr. Anderson to be a candid and sincere individual. I believe Mr. Anderson has done his best to provide an accurate account of his conditions. Mr. Anderson's subjective complaints matched my objective findings in my evaluation of him. In summation, I find no basis for questioning Mr. Anderson's credibility. It is evident he has suffered dental related injuries as a result of his industrial injuries.
Treatment Plan for Ronald Anderson

Please Note: I researched the ACOEM guidelines, the MTUS guidelines, and the ODG guidelines, and the recommended treatment plan is consistent with these evidence-based guidelines. Section 4604.5(d) of the Labor Code states that the treating physician is presumed to be the treating doctor for the purposes of this section, and is therefore authorized to make all necessary medical determinations.
The dental specialty of Orofacial Pain has been established by the American Dental Association, and as such, the treatment recommendations are within the standard of care for this specialty.

Mr. Anderson's diagnosed condition of bruxism and inflammation of the gums necessitates a comprehensive treatment plan. The primary purpose of the Orofacial Pain Occlusal Orthotic Device is to relieve facial muscle, ligament, and nerve impingement associated with his TMJ symptoms and bruxism behavior. This appliance is fabricated and customized in our in-house dental laboratory. The recommended treatment includes an Orofacial Pain Occlusal Orthotic Device (CPT code: 21085) to be worn during the day to alleviate his facial muscle pain and myalgia.
Due to Mr. Anderson's reported bruxism behavior, a Nocturnal Orthopedic Repositioning Device (CPT code: 21110) is also recommended to mitigate nighttime teeth grinding.

Bacteria and inflammatory processes from inflammation of the gums readily enter the bloodstream, potentially complicating his pre-existing diabetes. Therefore, Mr. Anderson will require monitoring throughout his lifetime with treatment as necessary on an industrial basis. Regular periodontal evaluations and maintenance are recommended.
To address his facial muscle pain and myalgia, physical medicine modalities and orthotic training are also recommended.
Given the presence of emotional stressors, a psychological examination is warranted to further assess and manage his condition.
Duration of Treatment Frequency of Treatment: 3 months, 1 time per month
Estimated date of completion of treatment: April 15, 2026

Mr. Anderson is Temporarily Partially Disabled. The condition is not Permanent and Stationary. Since Mr. Anderson suffers from Myalgia of the facial musculature, activities should be prophylactically restricted to avoid exacerbation. Work restrictions will be re-evaluated at each follow-up visit.

PLEASE NOTE: Labor Code 5402 (b)(c), requires the employer to authorize all appropriate medical care up to $10,000. I request that the claims adjuster please provide copies of all medical records and authorize the recommended treatment plan, including the Orofacial Pain Occlusal Orthotic Device (CPT code: 21085) and Nocturnal Orthopedic Repositioning Device (CPT code: 21110).
I request that the claims adjuster please provide copies of all medical records.

In accordance with Labor Code Section 4628, I solely performed the interview with Mr. Anderson. I solely performed the physical examination and I solely took his history of injury and medical history. I performed the examination in its entirety at my office. I reviewed the medical records and personally reviewed and signed this report.

To the best of my knowledge, the evaluation and the time performing it were in accordance with the guidelines of the Industrial Medical Council of Administrative Director to the extent that those guidelines exist.
I did not bill for any other medical-legal evaluations or diagnostic procedures or diagnostic services performed by independent contractors.
